title: Finishing floorboards with hard-wax oil
subtitle: Apply a penetrating hard-wax oil to sanded bare floorboards for a natural, repairable finish that feeds the wood
excerpt: How to finish bare floorboards with hard-wax oil. Covers sanding to the correct grit, applying two coats by cloth or pad, buffing between coats, and the maintenance schedule for keeping the finish in good order.
sourceNotes: Hardwax oil finishing technique from UK flooring trade practice. Osmo Polyx Oil, Rubio Monocoat, and Treatex technical application notes (generic referenced principles, not brand instructions).

glossaryTerms.definition: A penetrating wood finish made from vegetable oils (linseed, sunflower, soya) combined with hard waxes (carnauba, candelilla). Unlike surface-film finishes such as polyurethane or lacquer, hard-wax oil soaks into the wood surface rather than sitting on top of it, so there is no film to peel or chip. Damage can be spot-repaired by sanding the damaged area and re-oiling; the repair blends in. Two thin coats is the standard application; heavy coats do not penetrate and are left as a smear on the surface.

content.text: is a penetrating finish for bare floorboards: the oil feeds into the surface fibres and the wax sits in the pores rather than on top of them. It gives a natural, flat look with a surface that can be spot-repaired without sanding the whole floor. The approach is two thin coats with a cloth or short-nap pad, buffed between coats.

Hard-wax oil goes onto bare wood or wood sanded back to the level where no old finish remains. Start with a drum sander at 40-grit if removing old lacquer or varnish; finish at 80-grit or 100-grit. Do not go finer than 100-grit (hard-wax oil needs open grain to penetrate; sanding to 120-grit or finer closes the grain. Vacuum thoroughly and wipe with a just-damp cloth to raise the grain slightly, then sand at 80-grit with the grain before oiling.

Decant a small amount of oil into a tray. Apply with a short-nap roller, a lint-free cloth, or a wax applicator pad in the direction of the grain. Use less than you think) a thin coat is a coat that barely darkens the wood. Work in strips the width of 2 to 3 boards. Once applied, buff the wet surface with a clean cloth immediately to spread any surplus and push oil into the grain. Surplus oil sitting on the surface will dry to a tacky smear. Allow to dry fully (typically 4 to 8 hours in normal conditions.
Lightly sand and apply the second coat

Lightly sand between coats with 120-grit on a sanding block to knock back any raised grain or small dust nibs. Vacuum and wipe. Apply the second coat as the first) thin, buffed immediately. Allow 8 to 24 hours before light foot traffic. Full cure is 3 to 5 days; do not put rugs or furniture pads down until fully cured.

An oiled floor needs maintenance that a lacquered floor does not (but the maintenance is simple. Clean with a pH-neutral floor cleaner. Re-oil high-traffic areas once a year: sand lightly with 120-grit, wipe off dust, apply one thin coat, buff immediately. Spot-damaged boards can be treated individually without affecting the surrounding finish.

attrs.symptom: Patches of tacky or sticky surface after the oil has dried
attrs.cause: Too much oil applied in those areas; the surplus has not penetrated and is drying on the surface.
attrs.fix: Apply a small amount of fresh oil to the tacky area (this softens the uncured surplus), then buff vigorously with a clean dry cloth immediately to lift it off. Do not sand tacky areas) it will clog the sandpaper.
